mission.title: Install and Configure Karmada on Kubernetes
mission.description: Karmada is an open-source multi-cloud, multi-cluster Kubernetes orchestration tool that enables seamless management of cloud-native applications across various Kubernetes clusters. Installing Karmada allows you to leverage its advanced scheduling capabilities and centralized management for improved application deployment and scaling.

steps.title: Add the Karmada Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Karmada Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration:

```bash
helm repo add karmada-charts https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karmada-io/karmada/master/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Karmada using Helm
steps.description:
Install Karmada in the karmada-system namespace using the official Helm chart. Replace the version with your desired release:

```bash
helm --namespace karmada-system upgrade -i karmada karmada-charts/karmada \
  --version=1.12.0 \
  --create-namespace
```

Alternatively, you can use the karmadactl CLI to initialize Karmada:

```bash
karmadactl init
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that all Karmada components are running in the karmada-system namespace:

```bash
kubectl get pods -n karmada-system
```

You should see pods for the karmada-apiserver, karmada-controller-manager, karmada-scheduler, karmada-webhook, and etcd.

steps.title: Register member clusters
steps.description:
After Karmada is installed, register your member clusters. You can use karmadactl to join a cluster:

```bash
karmadactl join <member-cluster-name> --kubeconfig=<karmada-kubeconfig> --cluster-kubeconfig=<member-kubeconfig>
```

Verify the member clusters:

```bash
kubectl get clusters --kubeconfig=<karmada-kubeconfig>
```

uninstall.title: Uninstall Karmada via Helm
uninstall.description:
Remove the Karmada Helm release:

```bash
helm uninstall karmada -n karmada-system
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and namespace
uninstall.description:
Remove Karmada CRDs and the namespace:

```bash
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd | grep karmada.io | awk '{print $1}')
kubectl delete namespace karmada-system
```

If you used karmadactl, run:

```bash
karmadactl deinit
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade Karmada
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade to a new version:

```bash
helm repo update
helm --namespace karmada-system upgrade karmada karmada-charts/karmada --version=<new-version>
```

troubleshooting.title: Pod Not Starting
troubleshooting.description:
If the Karmada pods are not starting, check the logs of the pods for errors:

```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n karmada-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Karmada API Not Accessible
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Karmada API, ensure that the Karmada API server is running and check your network policies:

```bash
kubectl get pods -n karmada-system
kubectl get svc -n karmada-system
```

troubleshooting.title: etcd Issues
troubleshooting.description:
If etcd pods are failing, check storage and resource availability:

```bash
kubectl describe pod -l app=etcd -n karmada-system
kubectl logs -l app=etcd -n karmada-system
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Karmada will result in all components (apiserver, controller-manager, scheduler, webhook, etcd) running in the karmada-system namespace. You can then register member clusters and deploy workloads across them using Karmada's PropagationPolicy.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a running Kubernetes cluster (v1.16+), Helm v3+, and kubectl installed and configured.
